questions.questionText: Normal saline (0.9% NaCl) is NOT an ideal resuscitation fluid because:
questions.optionB: It causes hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis when given in large volumes (154 mEq/L chloride vs plasma 100 mEq/L → dilutional acidosis)
questions.explanation: Core concept: NS has supraphysiological chloride (154 vs 100 mEq/L) → acidosis with large volumes. Why correct: 0.9% NaCl contains 154 mEq/L each of Na+ and Cl- (plasma Cl- is only ~100 mEq/L). Large volumes → hyperchloremia → non-anion gap metabolic acidosis → renal vasoconstriction → AKI. Why A wrong: NS is actually cheap and widely available. Why C wrong: NS contains NO potassium (Ringer's lactate/Hartmann's contains 5 mEq/L K+). Why D wrong: NS is ISOTONIC (308 mOsm/L). Clinical relevance: Balanced crystalloids (Ringer's lactate/PlasmaLyte) are preferred over NS for large-volume resuscitation (SMART/SALT-ED trials — less AKI, less acidosis). NS still used for: hyperkalemia (K+-free), cerebral edema (avoids hyponatremia), and mixing with blood products. Exam trap: Ringer's lactate is NOT used with blood transfusion (calcium chelates citrate anticoagulant → clots). Use NS for blood. Memory aid: 'Normal Saline = Not truly Normal (too much Chloride).' Reference: Aitkenhead – Fluid Therapy.
questions.conceptTag: Normal saline hyperchloremic acidosis balanced crystalloid

questions.questionText: The most serious immune-mediated complication of blood transfusion is:
questions.optionB: Acute hemolytic transfusion reaction (ABO incompatibility — intravascular hemolysis → fever, chills, flank pain, hemoglobinuria, DIC, renal failure, shock)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Acute hemolytic transfusion reaction from ABO mismatch = most dangerous. Why correct: ABO-incompatible blood → preformed IgM antibodies → complement activation → intravascular hemolysis. Features: fever, rigors, flank/chest pain, dark urine (hemoglobinuria), DIC, renal failure, cardiovascular collapse. Why A wrong: Febrile non-hemolytic — most common reaction (cytokines from WBCs), benign, self-limited. Why C wrong: Urticaria — common allergic reaction, treated with antihistamines, usually benign. Why D wrong: TRALI (Transfusion-Related Acute Lung Injury) — second most common cause of transfusion DEATH, but ABO mismatch is the most feared immediate complication. Clinical relevance: Management: STOP transfusion immediately, maintain IV access, send blood bank recross-match, supportive (fluids, vasopressors, diuretics). Root cause: clerical error (wrong patient/wrong blood). Exam trap: TRALI presents as acute respiratory distress within 6 hours — bilateral CXR infiltrates without fluid overload. Memory aid: 'ABO wrong = Acute Bad Outcome (hemolysis).' Reference: Aitkenhead – Blood Therapy.
